patches.note: EXP-134: Fix FAIL-004 AAO sigma/500hPa equivalence and add NOAA CPC citation
patches.find: 4. Roaring 40s 500hPa anomaly (W020 / FAIL-004). The dome predicted a 500hPa geopotential height anomaly ≥3% above climatology in the Roaring 40s (40–60°S) latitude band during the test window of March 12–19, 2026. The observed Antarctic Oscillation (AAO) index averaged +0.78σ during the test period (NCEP/CPC AAO index, week of 2026-03-12) — indicating unremarkable Southern Annular Mode conditions. A ≥3% geopotential height anomaly in the 40–60°S band is an extreme event that did not occur. The prediction clearly failed. The dome then retroactively labeled the original prediction "refined," arguing the one-week test window was too narrow for a signal with a 3-month natural timescale. This is a textbook case of moving the goalposts: the dome set the test window, the data came in below threshold, and the dome then declared its own window insufficient. The registry was subsequently changed to require minimum 3-month test windows for atmospheric indices — a rule that did not exist when the prediction was made and that conveniently prevents falsification of the next attempt. If a model's test windows are always adjusted after the fact to exclude failures, the testing framework produces a 100% pass rate by construction. Two additional layers compound this problem. First, the prediction criterion itself conflicts with how the dome measures the same phenomenon elsewhere: W020 specified “500hPa geopotential height ≥3% above climatology” as its threshold, but the Live Power Dashboard Domain 10 tracks the same Southern Hemisphere atmospheric circulation using “AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr” — a wholly different metric. When the dome uses incompatible thresholds for what is supposed to be the same underlying signal, the threshold selection is itself post-hoc. Second, the retroactive 3-month minimum window rule applies exclusively to atmospheric predictions — which happen to be the predictions that failed. Magnetic predictions with identically short test windows were counted as confirmed without any such requirement. A methodological rule that is enforced only against the outcomes that were already failures is not a rule; it is a verdict in disguise.
patches.replace: 4. Roaring 40s 500hPa anomaly (W020 / FAIL-004). The dome predicted a 500hPa geopotential height anomaly ≥3% above climatology in the Roaring 40s (40–60°S) latitude band during the test window of March 12–19, 2026. This is a high bar: at 50°S, the 500hPa climatological height is approximately 5,500m, so a ≥3% anomaly requires a sustained deviation of ~165m — an extreme event. The Antarctic Oscillation (AAO) index, a 700hPa-based proxy for Southern Annular Mode activity (NOAA CPC AAO daily index, week of 2026-03-12), averaged +0.78σ during the test period — indicating unremarkable conditions well within normal variability. Note that the AAO itself is defined on 700hPa height anomalies poleward of 20°S, not on 500hPa heights in the 40–60°S band the dome specified. Even using this generous proxy, the observed conditions fell far short; a genuine ≥3% anomaly at 500hPa in the target band clearly did not occur. The prediction failed. The dome then retroactively labeled the original prediction "refined," arguing the one-week test window was too narrow for a signal with a 3-month natural timescale. This is a textbook case of moving the goalposts: the dome set the test window, the data came in below threshold, and the dome then declared its own window insufficient. The registry was subsequently changed to require minimum 3-month test windows for atmospheric indices — a rule that did not exist when the prediction was made and that conveniently prevents falsification of the next attempt. If a model's test windows are always adjusted after the fact to exclude failures, the testing framework produces a 100% pass rate by construction. Two additional layers compound this problem. First, the prediction criterion itself conflicts with how the dome measures the same phenomenon elsewhere: W020 specified "500hPa geopotential height ≥3% above climatology" as its threshold, but the Live Power Dashboard Domain 10 tracks the same Southern Hemisphere atmospheric circulation using "AAO < 0σ while SAA decay >50 nT/yr" — a wholly different metric at a different pressure level. When the dome uses incompatible thresholds for what is supposed to be the same underlying signal, the threshold selection is itself post-hoc. Second, the retroactive 3-month minimum window rule applies exclusively to atmospheric predictions — which happen to be the predictions that failed. Magnetic predictions with identically short test windows were counted as confirmed without any such requirement. A methodological rule that is enforced only against the outcomes that were already failures is not a rule; it is a verdict in disguise.
patches.rationale: EXP-134: Removes unjustified AAO σ ↔ 500hPa% equivalence. AAO is defined on 700hPa anomalies south of 20°S (not 500hPa 40-60°S band). Added explicit NOAA CPC citation and noted pressure-level mismatch which strengthens the argument. Also adds quantitative 165m deviation threshold context.

patches.note: Fix 'mid-afternoon' to 'late afternoon' in SEC-2.4 TLDR
patches.find: shrink by 52% between noon and mid-afternoon. Measured solar diameter is constant to 0.01%.
patches.replace: shrink by 52% between noon and late afternoon (6 PM at 90° hour angle). Measured solar diameter is constant to 0.01%.
patches.rationale: The 52% figure derives from a 90° hour angle = 6 PM, not mid-afternoon (2–4 PM). Correcting 'mid-afternoon' to 'late afternoon' removes the factual mislabeling.

patches.note: Fix 'mid-afternoon' to 'late afternoon' in SEC-2.4 body text
patches.find: apparent size between noon and mid-afternoon.
patches.replace: apparent size between noon and late afternoon.
patches.rationale: Consistent fix with TLDR: 'mid-afternoon' is factually wrong for the 52% figure (which is 6 PM).

patches.note: Fix imprecise atmospheric forcing mechanism description in WIN-058b
patches.find: shared atmospheric forcing (thermospheric expansion drives both SG loading and ionospheric currents)
patches.replace: shared electromagnetic forcing (extreme geomagnetic storms simultaneously affect magnetometers via field variation and superconducting gravimeters via ground-induced current artifacts)
patches.rationale: Thermospheric expansion is not what links SG signals to geomagnetic storms. The mechanism is electromagnetic: storm-time field variations and ground-induced currents. Using 'electromagnetic' is more accurate and removes the conflation.

patches.note: Fix stale κ = 1.672 cross-reference in WIN-058b (should be 1.677)
patches.rationale: The corrected κ value is 1.677 throughout the analysis; this cross-reference still had the old value 1.672.
